title: Suggested new alias
body:
I am not sure how this would look, but I almost always use this same kubectl command on most clusters.  It retrieves the dashboard login secret token from the K8s cluster.  It would be nice to see if there was a way to get it added into the regular plugin so I don’t need to add it every time, and others can benefit also:

Kubernetes Get Dashboard Token:
`alias kgdt=‘kubectl get secret -n kubernetes-dashboard $(kubectl -n kubernetes-dashboard get sa/admin-user -o jsonpath=“{.secrets[0].name}”) -o go-template=“{{.data.token | base64decode}}” | pbcopy; echo Dashboard Token added to clipboard’`

This requires the dashboard set up in the standard way according to Kubernetes directions, with the default access account, so it is not appropriate for all users, but hopefully somebody can do something with this code

title: How to suppress AWS profile info on right side of screen?
body:
I'm currently using the agnoster theme with the aws plugin.  This plugin helps display your current aws profile information in the command prompt.  This is working well and I appreciate it.

What I don't like is that on the far right of the screen, I get the aws profile info displayed again as "<aws:someProfileName>"

If I set SHOW_AWS_PROMPT=false, the info in the command prompt and the info pinned at the far right both disappear.  Any idea how to remove just the aws profile info at the right of the screen?

Looking at this further, the aws plugin is setting the RPROMPT value ([see here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/aws/aws.plugin.zsh)):

```
# AWS prompt
function aws_prompt_info() {
  [[ -n "$AWS_PROFILE" ]] || return
  echo "${ZSH_THEME_AWS_PREFIX:=<aws:}${AWS_PROFILE:gs/%/%%}${ZSH_THEME_AWS_SUFFIX:=>}"
}

if [[ "$SHOW_AWS_PROMPT" != false && "$RPROMPT" != *'$(aws_prompt_info)'* ]]; then
  RPROMPT='$(aws_prompt_info)'"$RPROMPT"
fi
```

title: how do i configure omz and virutalenv with a boot script?
body:
hello all, i'm trying to configure a remote machine with a script such that when i run a script, it creates a virtual environment with python 3.7.5 and it configures a bare oh-my-zsh install with virtualenv


the initial oh-my-zsh config w/o conmments is 

```shell
export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"

ZSH_THEME="robbyrussell"

plugins=(git)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

and the boot script runs in the project directory is 

```shell
#!/usr/bin/env zsh
set -euo pipefail

#  hack to workaround bug with homebrew configuration in this env
sudo chown -R brev /home/linuxbrew
echo 'eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> /home/brev/.zshrc
eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)"


# install and  configure pyenv to get python 3.7.5
brew install pyenv
echo 'export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"' >> ~/.zshrc
echo 'eval "$(pyenv init -)"' >> ~/.zshrc
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"    # if `pyenv` is not already on PATH
eval "$(pyenv init --path)"
eval "$(pyenv init -)"
pyenv install -s 3.7.5

# install virtualenv and virtualenvwrapper
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential python3.8-venv python3-pip python3-distutils python3-apt
echo "export PATH=/home/brev/.local/bin:\$PATH" >> ~/.zshrc
export PATH=/home/brev/.local/bin:$PATH
pip3 install virtualenv
pip3 install virtualenvwrapper

# overwrite user plugin to include virtualenvwrapper 
sed -e '/plugins/ s/^#*/#/' -i ~/.zshrc
sed -e '/source/ s/^#*/#/' -i ~/.zshrc
echo "plugins=(git virtualenvwrapper)" >> ~/.zshrc
echo "source \$ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh" >> ~/.zshrc

# reload zsrhrc
exec zsh

# create the virtualenv
mkvirtualenv --python=python3.7.5 -a `pwd` hn
```

python is installed properly, and the shell is configured properly when I ssh in (i can use workon and mkvirtualenv), but the virtualenv that i wanted to create in the setup script does not exist.

Is there a way that I can load that plugin into the current shell such that mkvirtualenv runs correctly? also the above script does not error, but should be since `mkvirtualenv` appears to fail.

title: My `.zshrc` was executed twice
body:
Recently when I use `diff` it always complains that `color-diff:1: maximum nested function level reached; increase FUNCNEST?`. And `which color-diff` gave me:

```console
$ which color-diff
color-diff () {
        color-diff --color $@
}
```

It's a weird recursive call. Eventually I found out that the cause was my `.zshrc` being executed twice, if I add `echo start` to the beginning of the `.zshrc` and `echo end` to the end, then run `omz reload` I will get:

```console
$ omz reload
start
end
start
end
```

If I add `which color-diff` to `.zshrc`, I will get:

```console
$ omz reload
color-diff () {
        diff --color $@
}
color-diff () {
        color-diff --color $@
}
```

{

            ;;

}

author: paultheman

title: Add descriptions for plugins?
body:
I'm a newbie to OMZ. I find your list of plugins to be really interesting, but also rather intimidating. I have no idea of what each one does or which ones I should install. I could click through each link, of course, but with ~100 different options, that's not realistic. I've come across this page several times, and each time I conclude, "this is too complicated" and skip installing plugins.

Some of the underlying packages are self-evident, like "Python." However, most of these names mean nothing to me. E.g., what does "autojump" do? 

Even the description after I click through is rather bare: "This plugin loads the [autojump navigation tool](https://github.com/wting/autojump)." This sounds intriguing, but it isn't informative unless (a) you already know what autojump does or (b) you have the time to click through to the underlying package and read the detailed description.

Would it be possible to expand this page with a description for each plugin and the underlying package? The descriptions would ideally have a one or two sentence summary about "why should I care?" -- or "what makes this awesome?" I.e., more than "this plugin loads X" or "this plugin adds syntax highlighting for Y." Rather, there would ideally be a short summary of the underlying package, like "Autojump is X and does Y." Perhaps a table format would work better, not a list.

I'd be happy to volunteer to write a first pass and submit a pull request, although it would require some research and time. I wouldn't want to do this unless there was buy-in. It would help if anyone could volunteer to review a first draft before I submit a formal pull request.

Just my two cents. Thanks for maintaining a great package!

PS: Is there a correct pronunciation for "ohmyzsh"? In my head, it's "oh my zosh" (like the English name"Josh"), but I realized that "zash," "zesh," "zish" or "zush" are also possibilities. The last two are hard to pronounce.  :-)

body:
I use the [alien theme](https://github.com/eendroroy/alien) and share my config via dotfiles across multiple systems.

On two of them the git information has stopped showing without any apparent change in the configuration.

I set the theme from `~/.zshrc` with...

```
if [ "${HOST}" = "host1" ] || [ "${HOSTNAME}" = "host1" ]; then
    export ALIEN_THEME="soft"
elif [ "${HOST}" = "host2" ] || [ "${HOSTNAME}" = "host2" ]; then

elif [ "${HOST}" = "host5" ] || [ "${HOSTNAME}" = "host5" ]; then
    export EMACS_SOCKET="/run/user/1000/emacs/server"
    export ALIEN_THEME="gruvbox"
fi

ZSH_THEME="alien/alien"
```

The `$PROMPT` now differs between systems. On one where the git info is still shown it is...

```
%F{232}%K{%(?.226.196)}%(?.. %? )%k%f%F{%(?.226.196)}%K{22}%k%f%F{254}%K{22} 8:02:08 am UTC %k%f%F{22}%K{28}%k%f%F{254}%K{28} 0 %k%f%F{28}%K{34}%k%f%F{232}%K{34} user1 %k%f%F{34}%K{82}%k%f%F{232}%K{82} %~ %k%f%F{82}%K{238}%k%f%F{154}%K{238} G  master %k%f%F{238}%K{244}%k%f%F{255}%K{244} @ %k%f%F{244}%K{253}%k%f%F{16}%K{253} %F{65}+%f %F{236}+%f %F{65}⭑%f %F{236}⭑%f %F{65}-%f %F{236}-%f %F{236}?%f %k%f%F{253}%K{235}%k%f%F{228}%K{235} %k%f%F{235}%f
%F{245}192.168.1.45 %f%F{228}%B❱%b %f
```

On a system where it has disappeared it reads...

```
%F{0}%K{%(?.87.202)}%(?.. %? )%k%f%F{%(?.87.202)}%K{229}%k%f%F{0}%K{229} 8:13:32 am GMT %k%f%F{229}%K{193}%k%f%F{0}%K{193} 0 %k%f%F{193}%K{87}%k%f%F{0}%K{87} user1 %k%f%F{87}%K{45}%k%f%F{0}%K{45} %~ %k%f%F{45}%K{241}%k%f%F{87}%K{241} %k%f%F{241}%f
%F{238}192.168.1.1 %f%F{87}%B❱%b %f

I've run `upgrade_oh_my_zsh` / `omz upgrade` on both systems and the `~/.oh-my-zsh/` repositories are both at `HEAD` on commit `f9bbf604`.

I've gone into `~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/theme/alien` and made sure its up-to-date.

If I check the value of `$ALIEN_SECTIONS_LEFT` it includes `vcs_branch:async` `vcs_status:async`  and `vcs_dirty:async` 

```
 echo $ALIEN_SECTIONS_LEFT 
exit time battery user path vcs_branch:async vcs_status:async vcs_dirty:async versions:async newline ssh venv prompt

I've tried with a couple of other included themes such as `robbyrussell` and `maran` and the git status is shown in those prompts so I'm unsure why they're not showing for the Alien theme (which looking at the git history hasn't been updated in some time), so I'm unsure why they're not showing. Perhaps something has changed in the `oh-my-zsh` framework that needs updating?

Any suggestions on how to recover this would be very much appreciated, I like the Alien theme because of the ability to have different coloured themes on a per host basis as it allows me to quickly see without having to read the prompt which host I'm on.

title: how should a omz plugin correctly set its completion?
body:
I am trying to fix tailscale plugin, but facing some problem. I have some basic understanding of how zsh completion works, and knows a little about "zcompdump", but just a little. I am using macos app store version of tailscale. So the executable is not `tailscale`, instead it is an alias pointing to an executable inside a macos app `/Applications/Tailscale.app/Contents/MacOS/Tailscale`. It is upper case "T" and it reveals this problem. tailscale plugin is very short, it checks whether completion file exists and bind completion file to command: (And I saw many other plugins has about the same structure like this) ```sh # If the completion file doesn't exist yet, we need to autoload it and # bind it to `tailscale`. Otherwise, compinit will have already done that. if [[ ! -f "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_tailscale" ]]; then typeset -g -A _comps autoload -Uz _tailscale if (( $+commands[tailscale] )); then _comps[tailscale]=_tailscale elif (( $+aliases[tailscale] )); then _comps[${aliases[tailscale]:t}]=_tailscale fi fi tailscale completion zsh >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_tailscale" &| ``` According to comment above (the comment also shows up in many other plugins), I should set `_comps` only once and after the first run, compinit will take care of it. This is where things get tricky. The first time this script runs (after remove `_tailscale` completion file), everything works smoothly. But then I close and reopen the terminal, auto completion is broken: ```sh > rm $ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_tailscale # 1. close and reopen a terminal, now the plugin script is "run for the first time" ❯ echo $_comps[tailscale] ❯ echo $_comps[Tailscale] _tailscale # now it is correct, I can use auto completion. # But now the `_comps` in .zcompdump file does not contains `_comps[Tailscale]=_tailscale`, this binding is not persisted # Now .zcompdump file only have a `#omz fpath: /Users/huyufan/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/tailscale` related to tailscale # 2. close and reopen a terminal again, this time _tailscale completion file already exists ❯ echo $_comps[tailscale] _tailscale ❯ echo $_comps[Tailscale] # It broke. And now there is a key-value: `'tailscale' '_tailscale'` in the .zcompdump file (lower case "t") ``` I have tried manually editing the .zcompdump file to make the binding `_comps[Tailscale]=_tailscale`, this could solve the problem. But it is definitely not the right way. I also tried removing my .zcompdump file, and ohmyzsh will generate a new one. It is done here with `compinit`, after fpath prepared(L90-L98), before plugin loaded(L203-L206): https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/6e9cda3d30d8e73c11e4d32044b7f4c5e06f822d/oh-my-zsh.sh#L124-L133 So it seems that whatever I do in plugin script will not affect what will be persist to .zcompdump file? And thus how should I save the binding `_comps[Tailscale]=_tailscale`(upper case)? Also, I don't know why it eventually became `_comps[tailscale]=_tailscale`(lower case). Is `compinit` takes the name of "tailscale" of the completion file (pure guess. I don't know)?

title: My first plugin `ctrl-branch`
body:
https://github.com/yakshaveinc/linux/tree/master/.oh-my-zsh

Pressing <kbd>Ctrl+B</kbd> to switch to `git` branch with single letter shortcut.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f40aa088-4818-48ef-a95c-982e9ddd051b)

---

### Installing

```sh
ln -s $PWD/ctrl-branch ~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/plugins/

omz plugin enable ctrl-branch 
```
### Changelog

The first version of the plugin was very simple.

```sh
ctrl-branch-command() {
  git branch -vvv
}

# bind ctrl-b to type string
bindkey -s '^b' 'ctrl-branch-command\n'
```

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c5a8f852-db32-4097-9d6d-3705c4e04279)

The first version just showed branches.
Second allowed to switch branch using menu line number.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/078124f6-8191-4672-844f-536bbd17efd6)

Third version calculates shortcut from the first available letter of branch name.
Fourth version highlights shortcuts inline.

title: installing ohmyzsh messed up my ssh sessions on the remote machine
body:
I just put ohmyzsh on a remote debian machine. Since I did it, there have been severe problems with the way the remote shell works via ssh. 
If I try to delete a word that I have typed using the backspace key, instead of moving the caret backwards and deleting what's on screen, the caret moves forwards. Though on the remote machine it is interpreting the keystrokes correctly. So for example I type
```
ls foo/bar
```
and then hit the backspace key three times to delete `bar` the screen on my local machine will show 
```
ls foo/bar   |
```
where `|` is the cursor. If execute the command it will execute `ls foo`, but it's up to me to count keystrokes to judge what I've actually typed.
If I use the arrow keys to move around it's even worse. If I typed 
```
ls foo/bar←←←
``` 
(hitting the left arrow key to go back three chars) and then type
```
baz/
```
(to insert the baz/ directory between foo and bar) the screen will show `ls foo/barbbarabarzbar/bar`, but the command `ls foo/baz/bar` will be executed. It's basically adding the remainder of the linbe after the cursor on each keystroke.
I tried switching back to bash on the remote machine, restarting the remote machine and local terminal emulator, but nothing. 
Remote machine is #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.128-1 (2025-02-07) x86_64, local machine is Arch, I'm using Kitty with zsh locally.

title: Warp terminal - `omz update` process doesn't seem to "exit" when complete
body:
howdy, I'm posting this here instead of the "Issues" list because I'm not sure if it's a bug in OMZ, or just a quirk of using it in Warp terminal specifically.. I have tried searching through the existing "Issues" and "Discussions", but haven't found a clear answer yet.

when I run `omz update`, OMZ does indeed update correctly, and it *doesn't* lock up my terminal.. but it doesn't ever seem to finish running.

for those of you who aren't familiar with it, Warp breaks down each command execution into a "block", showing a horizontal divider between commands. this is where we see the first indication that something is different when I run `omz update`, as per the following screenshot:

![Screenshot 2025-03-13 at 09 14 04](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d6f6f3b8-36ad-47bf-8c53-c3ba5e9dd602)

to highlight a few key points:

- `omz theme list` worked correctly, it showed my list of available themes and quit, with Warp showing the end of that block.
- `omz help` also worked correctly, with Warp again showing the end of that block.
- `omz version` also worked correctly.
- `omz update` updated OMZ with no issues, but it did not quit after running, so Warp still thinks the current block is active (mo horizontal divider).

at this point, my terminal is **not** locked, so I can continue running commands, as seen by the `omz version` command run immediately after updating.. but Warp still thinks I'm inside the execution block of the `omz update` command.

if I try and close this tab in Warp, macOS asks me if I really want to quit, because "You have 1 process running". selecting "Show running processes" from that dialog shows me the following:

![Screenshot 2025-03-13 at 09 21 12](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d1b3c48a-cb29-4403-a530-12aec1a51354)

hitting <kbd>Enter</kbd> on that entry doesn't do anything, <kbd>ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>c</kbd> doesn't do anything, `fg` says "no current job", my only option is to kill this tab in Warp and launch a new one.. and I'm not even sure if doing that *actually* ends the process left behind by `omz update`.

so... what's going on here then? is there something wrong with how `omz update` works? is this some random quirk with how Warp does things?

title: jj plugin's prompt and falling back to git prompt
body:
Hi,

Since the introduction of the JJ plugin, I have been using a prompt as recommended in the README and inspired by https://github.com/nasso/omzsh/blob/main/themes/sunakayu.zsh-theme (mentioned in the README).

I used the following theme:
```zsh
# copied from robbyrussell
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX="%{$fg_bold[blue]%}git:(%{$fg[red]%}"
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX="%{$reset_color%} "
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_DIRTY="%{$fg[blue]%}) %{$fg[yellow]%}%1{✗%}"
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_CLEAN="%{$fg[blue]%})"

_prompt_status() {
  local ref='format_short_id(self.change_id())'

  jj_prompt_template_raw "\" %{$fg_bold[blue]%}jj:(%{$fg[red]%}\" ++ if(self.empty(), \"%{$fg[blue]%}\", \"%{$fg[magenta]%}\") ++ $ref ++ \"%{$fg[blue]%})%{$reset_color%}\"" || git_prompt_status
}
_omz_register_handler _prompt_status
_prompt_status_async() {
  echo -n $_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[_prompt_status]
}

_prompt_info() {
  jj_prompt_template 'surround(" [", "]", self.description().first_line())' || git_prompt_info
}
_omz_register_handler _prompt_info
_prompt_info_async() {
  echo -n $_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[_prompt_info]
}

PROMPT='%(?:%{$fg_bold[green]%}%1{➜%} :%{$fg_bold[red]%}%1{➜%} ) %{$fg[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}$(_prompt_status_async)%{$reset_color%}$(_prompt_info_async) '
```

But unfortunately, this seems to break git_prompt_status and git_prompt_info, even when I call them by themselves, they just return nothing. I am at loss as to how fix this, or even investigate where is the problem. Any idea is welcome :)

I also tried with simpler version without async handlers and the same happens.

title: eval: command not found issue in custom function
body:
Hi, recently I tried to define a function to ease my effort to create a version upgrading commit in upstream repo.

 My problem is that once I execute `upstream engine`, the eval reports error:
```
(eval):1: command not found: go
_z:39: command not found: date                                                                                                           
_z:64: command not found: env
```
And then the zsh plugin features got broken, so I have had to rerun `~/.zshrc` to restore it.
Note that I found if I source `~/.zshrc` before eval it works, but it's very slow and i don't think that's a good way.
I don't know why eval causes this problem, and what should I do to fix it. Thanks a lot.

```diff
+	source ~/.zshrc
	# 3. Run the output of gg script
	eval "$gg_script"  || return 1
```

#### `~/.zshrc`

```diff
+ source ~/.config/own_cfg/git.sh
```

#### new added function `upstream`.

```
#!/usr/bin/env zsh

function module {
	remote=$(git remote get-url --push origin | sed 's/^gitlab@//; s/:/\//; s/\.git$//')
	echo "${remote}"
}

function revision {
	tag=$(git tag --points-at HEAD | head -n1)
	if [ -n "$tag" ]; then
		rev="$tag"
	else
		rev=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
	fi
	echo "$rev"
}

function gg {
	# Try to get the tag pointing to HEAD, otherwise use commit hash
	rev="$(revision)"
	echo "go get ${remote}@${rev}" | clipcopy
}

function project_name {
	remote=$(git remote get-url --push origin | sed 's/^gitlab@//; s/:/\//; s/\.git$//')
	basename=$(basename "$remote")
	echo "$basename"
}

function upstream {
	if [ -z "$1" ]; then
		echo "Error: upstream path required."
		return 1
	fi
	module_name=$(module)
	rev=$(revision)  # Correctly call the revision function

	gg_script="go get ${module_name}@${rev}"

	# 1. Use z to jump, select first, run gg to get script
	path=$(z "$1" -l | head -n1 | awk '{print $2}')
	if [ -z "$path" ]; then
		echo "No matching path found for '$1'"
		return 1
	fi

	cd "$path" || return 1

	# source ~/.zshrc
	# 3. Run the output of gg script
	eval "$gg_script"  || return 1

	# 4. git status, commit if changed
	if git diff --quiet && git diff --cached --quiet; then
		echo "No changes to commit."
		return 0
	fi

	branch="upstream/$(basename "$module_name")/$rev"
	echo $PATH
	git checkout -b "$branch"
	git add .
	basename=$(basename "$module_name")
	git commit -S -m "upstream(${basename}): ${basename}@${rev}"
}
```

last_vcs_info=""


function pipestatus_parse {
  PIPESTATUS="$pipestatus"
  ERROR=0
  for i in "${(z)PIPESTATUS}"; do
      if [[ "$i" -ne 0 ]]; then
          ERROR=1
      fi
  done

  if [[ "$ERROR" -ne 0 ]]; then
      print "[%{$fg[red]%}$PIPESTATUS%{$fg[cyan]%}]"
  fi

# Combine it all into a final right-side prompt

function preexec() {
    last_run_time=$(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f\n", time')
}

function duration() {
    local duration
    local now=$(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f\n", time')
    local last=$1
    local last_split=("${(@s/./)last}")
    local now_split=("${(@s/./)now}")
    local T=$((now_split[1] - last_split[1]))
    local D=$((T/60/60/24))
    local H=$((T/60/60%24))
    local M=$((T/60%60))
    local S=$((T%60))
    local s=$(((now_split[2] - last_split[2]) / 1000000000.))
    local m=$(((now_split[2] - last_split[2]) / 1000000.))

    (( $D > 0 )) && duration+="${D}d"
    (( $H > 0 )) && duration+="${H}h"
    (( $M > 0 )) && duration+="${M}m"

    if [[ $S -le 0 ]]; then
        printf "%ims" "$m"
    else
        if ! [[ -z $duration ]] && printf "%s" "$duration"
        local sec_milli=$((S + s))
        printf "%.3fs" "$sec_milli"
    fi
}

function precmd() {
    RETVAL=$(pipestatus_parse)
    local info=""

    if [ ! -z "$last_run_time" ]; then
        local elapsed=$(duration $last_run_time)
        last_run_time=$(print $last_run_time | tr -d ".")
        if [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f\n", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 120 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then
            local elapsed_color="%{$fg[magenta]%}"
        elif [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f\n", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 60 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then
            local elapsed_color="%{$fg[red]%}"
        elif [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f\n", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 10 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then
            local elapsed_color="%{$fg[yellow]%}"
        else
            local elapsed_color="%{$fg[green]%}"
        fi
        info=$(printf "%s%s%s%s%s" "%{$fg[cyan]%}[" "$elapsed_color" "$elapsed" "%{$fg[cyan]%}]" "$RETVAL")
        unset last_run_time
    fi

    if [ -z "$info" -a ! -z "$last_vcs_info" ]; then
        custom_prompt="$last_vcs_info$base_prompt"
        return;
    fi

    if (( ${+VCS_PROMPT} )); then
        last_vcs_info=$($VCS_PROMPT)
        if [ ! -z "$last_vcs_info" ]; then
            [ -z "$info" ] && info=$last_vcs_info || info="$info$last_vcs_info"
        fi
    fi

    [ -z "$info" ] && custom_prompt="$base_prompt" || custom_prompt="$info$base_prompt"
}

function hg_prompt_info() {
    unset output info parts branch_parts branch

    local output=""
    if ! output="$(hg status 2> /dev/null)"; then
        return
    fi

    local info=$(hg log -l1 --template '{author}:{node|short}:{remotenames}:{phabdiff}')
    local parts=(${(@s/:/)info})
    local branch_parts=(${(@s,/,)parts[3]})
    local branch=${branch_parts[-1]}
    [ ! -z "${parts[3]}" ] && [[ "${parts[1]}" =~ "$USER@" ]] && branch=${parts[3]}
    [ -z "${parts[3]}" ] && branch=${parts[2]}

    if [[ ! -z "$output" ]]; then
        local color="%{$fg[red]%}"
    elif [[ "${branch}" == "master" || "${branch}" == "warm" ]]; then
        local color="%{$fg[yellow]%}"
    else
        local color="%{$fg[green]%}"
    fi

    print "%{$fg[cyan]%}[${color}${branch}%{$fg[cyan]%}]"
}

setopt PROMPT_SUBST
PROMPT='$custom_prompt'

title: robbyrussell theme relies on a weird ZSH formatting behaviour - is it normal?
body:
So I've been on a modified robbyrussell theme with the arrow removed for years, and then an update changed it back to standard robbyrussell. No biggie; I went in the theme file and saw this line:
`PROMPT="%(?:%{$fg_bold[green]%}%1{➜%} :%{$fg_bold[red]%}%1{➜%} ) %{$fg[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"`

I didn't remember how I modified it last time, or the syntax used, but I decided to try truncating it to:
`PROMPT="%{$fg[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"`

This almost achieved the desired result, except the directory name wasn't in bold. I asked Copilot why is that the case and why was the directory name bold before I truncated the prompt and it turned out the bold "leaked" from the arrow. And indeed when I removed the reset_color thing from the original prompt instead, my commands would become cyan and bold. For some reason, fg_bold overrides fg but there's no way to do it in the opposite direction other than resetting the colour.

To me this sounds like a hack and I had an urge to submit a PR to change it to
`PROMPT="%(?:%{$fg_bold[green]%}%1{➜%} :%{$fg_bold[red]%}%1{➜%} ) %{$fg_bold[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"`
instead, so that the bolding is explicitly defined.

But I'm new to this and don't know if it's normal to use hacks like this in OMZ themes. If it is, I'll just leave it at that. But if not, I'd make a PR.

So, is it normal to rely on the bold leaking in OMZ themes? Or is it a robbyrussell specific issue?

title: Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly
body:
Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly using the latest (and versions since at least 1.5 years ago) Oh My Zsh on zsh 5.9 included with macOS 15.6.1 (& with earlier macOS versions) or installed from Homebrew core (I presume that all distributions of zsh 5.9, as well as older versions of zsh,  suffer from the issue).

See <https://github.com/apple/swift-argument-parser/issues/631#issuecomment-3287779901>.

I don't know the exact requirements for the failure, but it seems like, if you try to complete after a single `-`, and if a short & long option designate the same option (it might occur for all shorts & longs being distinct, or for multiple shorts or multiple longs designating the same option, etc.), then the display of the candidates below the command line is visually messed up, either way each long, each short, and each description on its own line, repeated multiple times (I've seen 3 repeats, but maybe the repeat count differs under circumstances).

Candidates seem to be displayed correctly if you try to complete after `--`.

The problem goes away if you disable Oh My Zsh or all Oh My Zsh matchers for completions, e.g. via:

```shell
zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list ''
```

I've attached a completion script that suffers from this problem: [_mas.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22309822/_mas.txt) (I had to append `.txt` to attach it).

Is there any way that existing completion scripts can be made compatible with Oh My Zsh fuzzy matching? 

If so, how?

Either way, is there a GitHub issue that tracks fixing this?

Oh My Zsh should be robust enough to not mess up display of valid zsh completion candidates. If the issue cannot be fixed, then should fuzzy matching be removed? Either way, should fuzzy matching be disabled by default until the problem is fixed?

title: Plugin for nrfutil completion needs explicit .zshrc sourcing
body:
I want to create a plugin that enables `nrfutil` completion fucntionalities.  
For reference, `nrfutil` is a tool used with Nordic MCUs, and it comes with a completion aid for `zsh` specifically, as explained [here](https://docs.nordicsemi.com/bundle/nrfutil/page/nrfutil-completion/guides/autocompletion_setup.html).  

The specific instructions by the tool itself:  
```
❯ nrfutil completion install zsh
Add the following to your .zshrc file:

# From nrfutil completion install
[[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh"

It should be added before the zsh completion system is initialized in your .zshrc as it adds an entry to the zsh 'fpath' variable.

If the zsh completion system is not initialized in your .zshrc file, you need to add initialization code:

# Initialize zsh completion system
autoload -Uz compinit
compinit
```

(I attach an archive with the `zsh` folder mentioned here just above: [nrfutil_zsh.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24345805/nrfutil_zsh.tar.gz))

Now, if I add the line:
```
[[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh"
```
in the `.zshrc` before the `plugins=(...)` statement, it works as expected.  

However, I want to wrap this into a plugin, for better order and portability.  

I've made a `nrfutil` folder in `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins` and added a file `nrfutil.plugin.zsh` with content:
```
[[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh"
# Mark _nrfutil completion function for autoloading
autoload -Uz _nrfutil
```
then added `nrfutil` to the `plugins=(...)` statement.  

This works, but only if I explicitly call `source .zshrc` after a `zsh` is started.  

What am I missing? How can I prevent having to explicitly source `.zshrc` itself again after `zsh` starts?

title: ccline — type a thought at your zsh prompt, get an AI answer and run the command
body:
I built **[ccline](https://github.com/jianshuo/ccline)**, a zsh plugin that hooks into `command_not_found_handler` to bring Claude/Codex answers directly into your shell workflow.

**How it works:**
- Type a natural-language thought (2+ words that are not a real command) at your prompt
- Claude or Codex answers, rendered as Markdown
- Runnable commands from the answer appear in an arrow-key menu — press Enter to execute in your live shell
- Single-word inputs still pass through as normal typos (no performance hit)

```
$ how do I find files bigger than 100MB

    find . -maxdepth 1 -type f -size +100M

Commands found — ↑/↓ to choose, Enter to run, q to cancel:
 ❯ find . -maxdepth 1 -type f -size +100M
   ➤ Run all of them
   ✗ Cancel
```

**Install (one line):**
```sh
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jianshuo/ccline/v0.2.2/install.sh | bash
```

Requires zsh + claude or codex CLI (auto-detected). MIT license.

GitHub: https://github.com/jianshuo/ccline
author: jianshuo
